description: The provided image displays a "Check for updates" user interface element and does not contain scientific data from the research paper. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, the paper investigates a high-Al low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C) processed via hot rolling and intercritical annealing at 750°C for various durations (5–180 min). Key figures in the paper reportedly include engineering stress-strain curves (Fig. 6A1) showing yield strengths of 427–465 MPa and tensile elongations of 5–12%, with optimum properties (UTS 658 MPa, 12% elongation) at 60 min annealing. Microstructural characterization via SEM, EBSD, XRD, and APT reveals phase evolution from martensitic structures in the hot-rolled quenched (HRQ) condition to dual-phase ferrite-austenite microstructures with reverted austenite needles and globules after annealing. The text reports austenite volume fractions decreasing from 46% (5 min) to 30% (180 min), with maximum mechanical stability and TRIP effect at 60 min.

Figure 1 presents the integrated thermomechanical processing design and phase transformation validation for a high-Al (~5 wt.%) added low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C). Panel (a) illustrates the schematic processing route after casting: homogenization at 1100°C for 2 hours, hot forging with 50% thickness reduction in the range of 1050-800°C followed by air cooling, hot rolling with soaking at 1100°C for 2 hours and two-pass 50% reduction (with 5-minute reheating between passes) followed by water quenching, and final intercritical annealing at 750°C for variable durations (5-180 minutes) with air cooling. Panel (b) displays the Thermo-Calc equilibrium phase fraction plot (TCFE9 database), revealing that the dual-phase region (BCC_A2 ferrite + FCC_A1 austenite) spans approximately 650°C to 1350°C, which provides the theoretical basis for selecting 750°C as the intercritical annealing temperature. Panel (c) shows dilatometry heating-cooling curves of hot-forged samples (5 and 15 minute holding times), exhibiting only a slight deviation around 600°C (marked by black ellipse) attributed to carbide dissolution, with no significant length contraction indicating minimal ferrite-to-austenite transformation due to the stability of delta ferrite. Collectively, these figures establish the computational and experimental foundation for optimizing the thermomechanical processing window to control austenite stability and phase transformations.

This figure presents a comprehensive phase and microstructural characterization of high (~5%) Al-added low-density medium Mn steel in cast and hot-forged conditions. Panel (a) displays X-ray diffraction patterns confirming the presence of α-ferrite (δ/α, BCC) and γ-austenite (FCC) phases in both specimens, with specific diffraction peaks labeled including α₁₁₀, γ₁₁₁, γ₂₀₀, α₂₀₀, γ₂₂₀, α₂₁₁, γ₃₁₁, and γ₂₂₂. The caption states Rietveld refinement indicates approximately 26% austenite in the cast sample and 29% in the hot-forged sample. Panels (b) and (c) show optical micrographs of the cast specimen revealing a dual-phase microstructure featuring dendritic austenite (indicated by blue arrows) distributed within the ferrite matrix. Panels (d) and (e) present optical images of the hot-forged specimen, where residual dendritic structures remain visible (marked by blue arrows) despite partial dissolution during high-temperature soaking prior to forging. Panel (f) provides a scanning electron microscopy image of the hot-forged sample confirming elongated austenite (γ) grains within the δ-ferrite (δ) matrix. Panel (g) shows an EBSD phase map superimposed on the image quality (IQ) map, with red representing BCC (δ-ferrite) and green representing FCC (austenite), visually confirming the elongated austenite morphology. The figure collectively demonstrates that both processing states maintain a stable dual-phase microstructure of δ-ferrite and austenite, with the hot-forged specimen exhibiting slightly higher austenite volume fraction and elongated grain morphology compared to the as-cast dendritic structure.

Figure 3 presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of the hot rolled & quenched (HRQ) specimen of the high-Al low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C). Subfigure (a) shows an optical micrograph with grains elongated along the rolling direction (RD), revealing δ-ferrite and α/γ regions; the inset differential interference contrast (DIC) image confirms the phase boundaries. Subfigure (b) provides a scanning electron microscopy image displaying the δ-ferrite matrix and lenticular martensite formed within prior austenite (α/γ) due to water quenching from approximately 800°C. Subfigure (c) presents EDS analysis with an energy spectrum and quantitative composition table, indicating Mn enrichment in austenite/martensite (8.36±0.16 wt%) compared to δ-ferrite (7.02±0.25 wt%), while Al shows higher concentration in δ-ferrite (5.61±0.11 wt%) than in austenite/martensite (4.75±0.11 wt%). Subfigure (d) displays an EBSD phase map superimposed on the IQ map, distinguishing BCC (red, δ-ferrite and martensite) and FCC (green, retained austenite) phases, confirming the presence of approximately 19% untransformed austenite alongside thermally stable δ-ferrite and validating the martensitic transformation during quenching.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis illustrating the microstructural evolution of a high-Al-added low-density medium Mn steel during intercritical annealing at 750°C. The five sample groups (A-E) correspond to annealing times of 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes, respectively. Subfigures A1-E1 show lower-magnification SEM images revealing the distribution of stable δ-ferrite matrix and α/γ regions (tempered martensite/austenite), while A2-E2 provide higher-magnification views detailing morphological transformations. At 5 minutes (A2), sharp needle-shaped reverted austenite forms within prior martensite, measuring approximately 2.83±0.30 µm in length. As time increases to 60 minutes (C2), reverted austenite transforms from needle-like to globular morphology with aspect ratios increasing, while partially dissolving into intercritical ferrite. By 180 minutes (E2), reverted austenite almost completely dissolves, leaving mainly intercritical ferrite and untransformed austenite. Subfigures A3-E3 display EDS spectra and quantitative composition tables, revealing elemental partitioning: δ-ferrite maintains high Al (~5.2-5.9 wt%) and moderate Mn (~6.6-7.4 wt%), while reverted austenite becomes Mn-lean (7.6-8.3 wt%) compared to untransformed austenite (9.1-9.8 wt% Mn), confirming Mn partitioning to untransformed austenite during holding.

The figure presents nanoindentation load-displacement curves for Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel under different thermomechanical processing conditions, comprising nine subplots (A1-C3) each containing optical micrographs indicating the specific phases tested. The curves systematically compare three processing states: Hot Rolled & Quenched (HRQ), and intercritically annealed at 750°C for 5 minutes (IA750_5 Min) and 60 minutes (IA750_60 Min). Subplots A1-C1 target δ-ferrite, showing consistent load-displacement behavior with calculated hardness values of 4439±206 MPa, 4267±264 MPa, and 4150±200 MPa respectively, indicating minimal variation with annealing duration. Subplots A2-C2 characterize martensite (in HRQ) and reverted austenite (in annealed samples), revealing a dramatic hardness decrease from martensite (~8897±550 MPa, penetration depth ~384 nm at 30 mN load) to needle-shaped reverted austenite (3867±110 MPa at 5 min, further decreasing to 3478±334 MPa at 60 min), confirming the dissolution of hard martensite into softer reverted austenite. Subplots A3-C3 examine untransformed austenite, exhibiting maximum penetration depths around 550 nm and stable hardness values (~4080-4196 MPa) across all processing conditions. The data collectively demonstrate that while δ-ferrite and untransformed austenite maintain mechanical stability during intercritical annealing, reverted austenite exhibits progressively decreasing hardness with extended annealing time, supporting the phase transformation mechanism of martensite reversion and elemental partitioning during heat treatment.

This figure presents mechanical characterization of a high (~5%) Al-added low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C) processed via hot rolling (HR) and intercritical annealing at 750°C for various durations (5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes). Subfigures A1 and A2 display the engineering and true stress-strain curves, respectively, showing that annealing significantly improves ductility compared to the as-hot-rolled condition, with the 60-minute sample exhibiting the highest ultimate tensile strength (~658 MPa) and elongation (~12%). Subfigures B1-B4 illustrate the corresponding work hardening rate (WHR) curves as a function of true strain, each revealing two distinct deformation stages: S1 (sharp decrease associated with δ-ferrite deformation) and S2 (linear decrease due to competing softening and hardening mechanisms, specifically the TRIP effect from austenite-to-martensite transformation). The mean WHR in stage S2 increases with annealing time from 1100-400 MPa (5 min) to 2000-1000 MPa (60 min), indicating enhanced austenite stability and TRIP effect, before decreasing to 1500-900 MPa at 180 minutes due to austenite dissolution. These results demonstrate that 60-minute intercritical annealing optimizes austenite stability, maximizing the TRIP effect and achieving the best strength-ductility balance (highest PSE).

Figure 7 comprehensively characterizes phase evolution and mechanical stability in an Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C low-density steel intercritically annealed at 750°C. Subfigures (a) and (b) present XRD patterns (2θ: 40–120°) before and after tensile deformation, respectively, indexing α-ferrite (δ/α) and γ-austenite peaks across Hot Rolled and IA750 specimens (5–180 min). Subfigure (c) quantifies austenite volume fraction (black: pre-test, red: post-test), showing a decline from 46% (5 min) to 30% (180 min), with the transformation ratio (blue curve) peaking at 20% for IA750_60Min and mechanical stability K (green curve) exhibiting inverse correlation. Subfigure (d) plots yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), total elongation (TE), and product of strength and elongation (PSE), revealing optimal mechanical performance at 60 min (UTS ~658 MPa, TE ~12%). The figure establishes that 60 min annealing achieves optimal austenite stability for enhanced TRIP effect.

This figure presents a six-panel schematic illustration depicting the dissolution and transformation mechanisms of martensite into reverted austenite and intercritical ferrite during intercritical annealing at 750°C in a high Al-added (~5%) low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C). Panel (a) shows the initial hot-rolled and quenched (HRQ) condition containing lenticular martensite (α') surrounded by untransformed austenite (γ^o) within a delta ferrite (δ) matrix. Panels (b) through (f) sequentially illustrate the microstructural evolution at increasing annealing durations: 5 minutes shows formation of sharp needle-shaped reverted austenite (γ^rev) and intercritical ferrite (α) from martensite; 30 and 60 minutes demonstrate the morphological transition from needle-like to globular reverted austenite with decreasing aspect ratio; while 120 and 180 minutes show progressive dissolution of reverted austenite via elemental partitioning of Mn and Al to adjacent untransformed austenite. The schematic visually explains how annealing time controls morphology, size, and chemical composition of reverted austenite, correlating these features with mechanical stability. Specifically, the 60-minute condition represents an optimal balance between fine globular morphology and composition, providing optimum mechanical stability for enhanced TRIP effect, whereas extended annealing to 180 minutes leads to complete dissolution and reduced TRIP capability.

This figure presents high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis of the IA750_60 Min specimen (intercritically annealed at 750°C for 60 min) from a high-Al low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C). Panel (a) shows a bright-field TEM image revealing nanoscale microstructural features including dark-contrast particles embedded in the matrix with visible dislocation networks. Panel (b) displays the corresponding HAADF-STEM image providing Z-contrast information with 500 nm scale bar. Panels (c) through (g) present individual EDS elemental mapping for Fe (red), Mn (green), Al (blue), Si (yellow), and C (cyan), respectively, while panel (h) shows the composite overlay of all elements on the HAADF image. The EDS maps demonstrate clear Mn enrichment and Al depletion in the reverted austenite regions compared to the surrounding intercritical ferrite matrix, confirming the dissolution of martensite into reverted austenite and α-ferrite during annealing. Notably, no evidence of precipitates (such as κ-carbides) or elemental clustering was observed, ruling out precipitation strengthening as the source of the highest product of strength and elongation (PSE) observed in this specimen.
